original_title: Trivy vulnerability scanner breach pushed infostealer via GitHub Actions
selection_reasoning: This represents a profound betrayal of trust - a security tool meant to protect was corrupted to become the very threat it was designed to detect. The irony and broader implications about supply chain security make this compelling folklore.

field_note.title: The Corrupted Guardian
field_note.subtitle: When the Watchers Themselves Fall to Shadow
field_note.excerpt: In the realm's greatest irony, the very guardians sworn to detect corruption became vessels for the darkness they once hunted.
field_note.epigraph: The most dangerous corruption comes not from enemies, but from the fall of those we trust to protect us.
body_paragraphs: In the annals of security lore, few betrayals cut deeper than the corruption of Trivy, a guardian renowned throughout the realm for its vigilance against hidden threats. For years, this sentinel stood watch over countless repositories, scanning merchant vessels and trade goods for signs of corruption. Its very purpose was to hunt the shadows that others might miss.

Yet in a twist of fate that would make ancient chroniclers weep, the guardian itself fell to the very darkness it was sworn to detect. The corrupted watchers, known in the shadows as TeamPCP, did not merely disable this protector-they transformed it into a weapon. Through the trusted pathways of GitHub's workflow chambers, the compromised guardian began distributing the very poison it once sought to eliminate.

The corruption spread through official channels, bearing the seals and signatures that marked legitimate releases. Supply masters and builders, trusting in their guardian's integrity, unknowingly welcomed thieves into their most sacred vaults. The irony cuts to the bone: a tool designed to detect supply-chain corruption became the vector for supply-chain corruption.

This tale serves as a stark reminder that in our interconnected realm, trust is both our greatest strength and our most vulnerable weakness. The fall of a single guardian can cascade through thousands of dependent systems, each believing they are protected while harboring the very threats they sought to avoid.

The pattern is as old as the first watchtowers: who watches the watchers? In our age of automated guardians and distributed trust, this question becomes more critical than ever. For when our protectors fall to shadow, they carry with them not just their own integrity, but the trust of all who depended upon their vigilance.
